decisions.question: GaryClaw on main branch. You have three pieces of work in flight: Phase 4b (cron scheduling), the Creative Oracle design (memory-backed decisions), and Parallel Daemons (multi-instance support). What should this CEO review focus on? If you pick a single design, I'll deep-dive it. If you pick 'all three,' I'll review them as a unified roadmap.

options.label: All three as roadmap (Recommended)
options.description: CEO review of the full next-phase strategy: 4b + Creative Oracle + Parallel Daemons as a unified vision. Best for prioritization and sequencing decisions.

options.label: Creative Oracle only
options.description: Deep-dive the memory-backed decision engine design (Phase 5). Already has a detailed draft.

options.label: Parallel Daemons only
options.description: Deep-dive multi-instance daemon support. Already has a detailed draft.

options.label: Phase 4b only
options.description: Focus on cron triggers and config hot-reload — the stated 'next step' in CLAUDE.md.

decisions.rationale: CEO review is a prioritization and sequencing exercise — reviewing all three together lets you make sequencing decisions, spot dependencies, and cut scope in one pass rather than three separate reviews.

decisions.question: Three ways to sequence this work. Think of it like building a race car: Approach A builds the engine first (scheduling + parallelism = infrastructure), Approach B builds the brain first (Oracle memory = intelligence), Approach C builds everything together as one integrated system. Which approach fits your priorities?

options.label: B) Brain-First (Recommended)
options.description: Creative Oracle (5a-5d) first, then 4b scheduling, then parallel daemons. Ships the differentiator first — the feature no other tool has. Effort: M overall. Risk: Low (Oracle is isolated, doesn't touch daemon lifecycle).
options.preview:
APPROACH B: Brain-First
  Sequence: Oracle Memory (5a) → Reflection (5b)
           → Domain Research (5c) → Taste (5d)
           → Cron Scheduling (4b)
           → Parallel Daemons
  Effort:  M (human: ~3 weeks / CC: ~6 hours)
  Risk:    Low — Oracle changes are isolated
  Pros:    • Ships the moat first
           • Oracle improves ALL subsequent daemon runs
           • Validated by 2026 SOTA (file-based memory)
  Cons:    • No scheduling until Oracle ships
           • Daemon stays manual-trigger for longer
  Reuses:  oracle.ts, job-runner.ts completion hooks

options.label: A) Infrastructure-First
options.description: 4b scheduling first, then parallel daemons, then Creative Oracle. Ships the 'just works' daemon features first. Effort: S-M overall. Risk: Low.
options.preview:
APPROACH A: Infrastructure-First
  Sequence: Cron Scheduling (4b) → Config Reload
           → Parallel Daemons
           → Creative Oracle (5a-5d)
  Effort:  S-M (human: ~2 weeks / CC: ~4 hours)
  Risk:    Low — incremental additions
  Pros:    • Daemon becomes useful fastest
           • Smallest first step (4b is XS)
           • Parallel daemons inform Oracle design
  Cons:    • Oracle (the differentiator) ships last
           • Stateless decisions for longer
  Reuses:  triggers.ts, daemon.ts, job-runner.ts

options.label: C) Unified Platform
options.description: Design all three together, ship as one integrated release. Cleanest architecture but longest time-to-first-ship. Effort: L. Risk: Medium (large blast radius).
options.preview:
APPROACH C: Unified Platform
  Sequence: Shared types + config schema
           → All three in parallel design
           → Integrated implementation
           → Single release
  Effort:  L (human: ~4 weeks / CC: ~8 hours)
  Risk:    Medium — large diff, complex testing
  Pros:    • Cleanest architecture from day 1
           • No retrofitting between phases
           • Shared config design
  Cons:    • Longest time to any value
           • Harder to test incrementally
           • Big-bang risk
  Reuses:  Everything, but all at once

decisions.rationale: CEO phase means P1+P2 dominate — Brain-First ships the differentiator (the feature no other tool has) first while keeping blast radius small, and its isolation means scheduling/parallel work can layer on cleanly after without rework.

decisions.question:
GaryClaw on main, reviewing the 3-phase roadmap (Oracle → Scheduling → Parallel). What review posture do you want? You already have detailed draft designs — this is about whether to push scope further or lock in what you have.

RECOMMENDATION: Choose SELECTIVE EXPANSION — the drafts are solid, but I spotted expansion opportunities worth surfacing (cross-project memory, Oracle self-improvement, taste inference). You cherry-pick what's worth adding. Completeness: EXPANSION=9/10, SELECTIVE=8/10, HOLD=7/10, REDUCTION=5/10.

options.label: SELECTIVE EXPANSION (Recommended)
options.description: Hold current scope as baseline, but surface expansion opportunities individually for cherry-picking. Neutral recommendations — you decide what's worth adding.

options.label: SCOPE EXPANSION
options.description: Dream big. Push scope UP. 10x check, platonic ideal, delight opportunities. Every expansion presented for opt-in.

options.label: HOLD SCOPE
options.description: The drafts are right. Maximum rigor on architecture, security, edge cases, observability. No expansions surfaced.

options.label: SCOPE REDUCTION
options.description: The roadmap is overbuilt. Find the minimum viable version and cut everything else.

decisions.rationale: The drafts are solid infrastructure — selective expansion lets us cherry-pick high-value additions like cross-project memory and taste inference without bloating scope, consistent with the brain-first sequencing we already committed to.

decisions.question:
EXPANSION 1: Cross-project memory sharing. Right now, the Creative Oracle's memory lives in `.garyclaw/oracle-memory/` per project. But domain expertise about 'autonomous agent architectures' learned in GaryClaw would be valuable in other agent projects. This would add a global `~/.garyclaw/oracle-memory/` layer that any project can read, with per-project overrides. 

Effort: S (human: ~3 days / CC: ~30 min). Risk: Low — additive, no breaking changes. Already called out as Open Question #1 in the Creative Oracle design.

options.label: A) Add to scope
options.description: Design the two-layer memory model (global + per-project) as part of Phase 5a. ~30 extra lines in oracle-memory.ts.

options.label: B) Defer to TODOS.md
options.description: Note as a future enhancement. Ship per-project memory first, add global layer later.

options.label: C) Skip
options.description: Not valuable enough to track.

decisions.rationale: It's low-effort (~30 lines), additive with no breaking changes, already identified as an open question in the design, and cross-project memory sharing is a natural completeness win for an autonomous agent that learns domain expertise.

decisions.question:
EXPANSION 2: Taste inference from decision patterns. The Oracle observes your decisions over time — after 50+ decisions, it notices patterns like 'you consistently choose the explicit approach over the clever one' or 'you always pick the complete option.' It suggests updates to taste.md: 'I noticed you prefer X over Y — should I add this to your taste profile?' You approve or reject each suggestion.

This is Open Question #2 in the Creative Oracle design. The risk noted there is 'Oracle gaming its own preferences' — but with human approval on every taste update, that risk is mitigated.

Effort: S (human: ~3 days / CC: ~30 min). Risk: Low — suggestions are human-gated, never auto-applied.

options.label: A) Add to scope
options.description: Add taste inference as Phase 5e — runs after 5b reflection, analyzes decision patterns, proposes taste.md updates with human approval.

options.label: B) Defer to TODOS.md
options.description: Track for later. Ship taste.md as human-authored only, add inference later.

options.label: C) Skip
options.description: Not valuable — human-authored taste is sufficient.

decisions.rationale: Human-gated taste inference closes the loop on the Oracle's learning cycle — it's in blast radius of the Creative Oracle design, low risk, and small effort, so it should ship with the phase.

decisions.question:
EXPANSION 3: Decision quality tracking. After each job, compare Oracle decisions against outcomes — did the chosen approach actually work? Track a rolling 'decision accuracy' metric: decisions that led to successful fixes vs. ones that needed revision. Display in `daemon status` and notification summaries. Over time, this gives you a trust score for the Oracle.

This is the missing feedback loop — reflection (5b) updates memory, but nothing measures whether the memory is actually making decisions *better*.

Effort: S (human: ~2 days / CC: ~20 min). Risk: Low — read-only metrics, no behavior changes.

options.label: A) Add to scope
options.description: Add decision accuracy tracking to Phase 5b reflection. Store metrics in oracle-memory/metrics.json, display in daemon status.

options.label: B) Defer to TODOS.md
options.description: Ship reflection first, add metrics later once there's enough decision data to be meaningful.

options.label: C) Skip
options.description: Not valuable — the reflection narrative is sufficient.

decisions.rationale: The feedback loop completes the reflection system — without measuring whether memory-backed decisions actually improve outcomes, you're flying blind on Oracle quality, and it's only ~20 min CC effort squarely in the blast radius of Phase 5b.

decisions.question:
EXPANSION 4: Memory-informed scheduling. Once the Oracle has memory, it can suggest *when* to run skills. Example: 'QA finds 3x more bugs after large commits — I should trigger QA after commits touching 5+ files, not on every push.' The daemon learns optimal trigger patterns from job outcomes rather than relying on static cron rules.

This would make Phase 4b's cron triggers a fallback rather than the primary scheduling mechanism.

Effort: M (human: ~1 week / CC: ~1 hour). Risk: Medium — requires enough job history to learn from, cold-start problem.

options.label: A) Add to scope
options.description: Design adaptive scheduling as Phase 4b+ — cron as baseline, memory-informed trigger suggestions layered on top.

options.label: B) Defer to TODOS.md
options.description: Ship static cron first. Add adaptive scheduling once there’s enough job history (50+ jobs) to learn from.

options.label: C) Skip
options.description: Static cron is sufficient. Don't over-engineer scheduling.

decisions.rationale: Medium effort + cold-start problem means this can't ship until there's enough job history anyway — ship static cron now, layer adaptive scheduling once 50+ jobs provide real data to learn from.

decisions.question:
EXPANSION 5: Memory conflict resolution protocol. Open Question #4 in the Creative Oracle design: when domain expertise says 'X is SOTA' but decision-outcomes shows 'X failed for us,' which wins? Rather than leaving this ambiguous, codify it as a 7th Decision Principle: 'Local evidence trumps general knowledge — if we tried X and it failed, prefer alternatives even if X is theoretically SOTA.'

This resolves the ambiguity before it causes a bad decision at 2am.

Effort: XS (human: ~2 hours / CC: ~10 min). Risk: None — it's a principle addition, not a code change.

options.label: A) Add to scope
options.description: Add the conflict resolution principle to oracle.ts and the Creative Oracle design. ~10 lines of prompt text.

options.label: B) Defer to TODOS.md
options.description: Note the ambiguity, resolve it after seeing real-world conflicts.

options.label: C) Skip
options.description: The Oracle will figure it out from context.

decisions.rationale: XS effort, zero risk, and it resolves a known ambiguity before it causes a bad autonomous decision — this is exactly the kind of blast-radius cleanup that should be done now.

decisions.question:
EXPANSION 6: Research freshness policy. Open Question #3 in the Creative Oracle design: how often should domain-expertise.md be refreshed? Rather than leaving it vague, codify a staleness check: domain-expertise.md has a `last_researched` timestamp. If older than 14 days AND the Oracle encounters a low-confidence decision in that domain, trigger an automatic refresh before the next job. Otherwise, manual via `garyclaw research <topic>`.

This prevents both 'stale knowledge' and 'unnecessary re-research.'

Effort: XS (human: ~2 hours / CC: ~10 min). Risk: None — simple timestamp check.

options.label: A) Add to scope
options.description: Add staleness check to Phase 5c (Domain Research). Timestamp in domain-expertise.md frontmatter, 14-day default, configurable.

options.label: B) Defer to TODOS.md
options.description: Ship manual-only research first, add automatic freshness check later.

options.label: C) Skip
options.description: Manual refresh is sufficient.

decisions.rationale: XS effort, zero risk, resolves Open Question #3 before it causes stale knowledge to produce a bad autonomous decision at 2am — same pattern as Expansion 5.

decisions.question:
GAP 1: If memory files have permission errors (EACCES) or disk is full (ENOSPC), the Oracle and Reflection will crash with unhandled exceptions. These are rare but real in daemon mode (long-running process, disk fills up overnight). The fix is a try/catch around all file I/O in oracle-memory.ts that degrades gracefully: log the error, continue without memory.

RECOMMENDATION: Choose A — this is a daemon running unattended at 2am. Silent crashes are the #1 thing to prevent. 5 lines of try/catch. Completeness: A=9/10, B=4/10.

options.label: A) Fix in plan (Recommended)
options.description: Add try/catch around all oracle-memory.ts file I/O. On EACCES/ENOSPC: log error, degrade to no-memory mode. ~5 lines per operation.

options.label: B) Accept risk
options.description: EACCES/ENOSPC are rare enough that crash + daemon restart is acceptable.

decisions.rationale: A daemon running unattended must never crash from predictable I/O errors — 5 lines of try/catch is the obvious, explicit fix that ensures completeness.

decisions.question:
GAP 2: If a cron expression in daemon.json is invalid (e.g., `"*/5 * * * * *"` with 6 fields or `"bad"` string), CronTrigger will throw a parse error at daemon startup, killing the entire daemon — including valid git poll triggers that were working fine.

RECOMMENDATION: Choose A — one bad cron expression shouldn't take down the whole daemon. Validate at config load time, skip invalid triggers with a warning. Completeness: A=9/10, B=5/10.

options.label: A) Validate + skip (Recommended)
options.description: Validate cron expressions during config load. Invalid expressions: log warning, skip that trigger, start daemon with remaining valid triggers.

options.label: B) Fail fast
options.description: Invalid cron = config error = daemon refuses to start. Forces the user to fix config before running.

decisions.rationale: A daemon running unattended must be resilient — one bad trigger shouldn't kill unrelated healthy triggers, consistent with the GAP 1 decision to degrade gracefully.

decisions.question:
SECURITY: Prompt injection via memory files. The reflection step writes to decision-outcomes.md based on job output. If a malicious repo tricks a skill into producing output containing prompt injection payloads, those payloads persist in memory and get injected into every future Oracle call. Similarly, domain-expertise.md is synthesized from web search results which could contain adversarial content.

This is a real but low-likelihood attack vector — the attacker would need to control either the repo being analyzed or the web search results.

RECOMMENDATION: Choose A — simple sanitization of memory file content before injection into the Oracle prompt. Strip known prompt injection patterns (e.g., 'ignore previous instructions'). Low effort, meaningful defense-in-depth. Completeness: A=8/10, B=6/10.

options.label: A) Add sanitization (Recommended)
options.description: Sanitize memory file content before Oracle prompt injection. Strip known injection patterns, cap per-file size. ~20 lines in oracle-memory.ts.

options.label: B) Accept risk
options.description: Low-likelihood vector. The Oracle runs in a sandboxed context anyway (no destructive tool access). Document as known risk.

decisions.rationale: Defense-in-depth for a daemon running unattended at 2am is worth ~20 lines — consistent with prior GAP decisions choosing completeness over accepted risk.

decisions.question:
Two minor edge cases in the cross-instance and config reload flows:

1. If a daemon instance crashes and leaves behind a corrupt `daemon-state.json`, `isSkillSetActive()` will throw when scanning that file — blocking dedup checks for all instances.

2. If `daemon.json` is corrupt when SIGHUP triggers a config reload, the daemon should keep the old config rather than crashing.

Both fixes are trivial try/catch patterns. No real alternatives here — I'll add both to the plan as error handling requirements.

RECOMMENDATION: Acknowledge and move on — these are obvious fixes, not decisions.

options.label: Acknowledged (Recommended)
options.description: Add try/catch for corrupt daemon-state.json in cross-instance scan and for corrupt config on SIGHUP reload. Both degrade gracefully.

options.label: Skip
options.description: These are rare enough to not worry about.

decisions.rationale: Consistent with GAP 1 and GAP 2 decisions — daemon resilience against corrupt files is exactly the same pattern we already approved, and these are trivial try/catch additions.

decisions.question:
DRY VIOLATION: The corruption recovery pattern (read JSON → parse fails → rename to .bak → try rotation) exists in `checkpoint.ts` and will be duplicated in `oracle-memory.ts`. Both modules need atomic write + corrupt recovery + rotation.

Extract a shared `src/safe-json.ts` utility with `safeReadJSON()` and `safeWriteJSON()` that both modules import. ~30 lines, eliminates the duplication.

RECOMMENDATION: Choose A — DRY is a stated engineering preference. Two copies of the same file corruption logic will drift. Completeness: A=9/10, B=5/10.

options.label: A) Extract shared utility (Recommended)
options.description: Create src/safe-json.ts with safeReadJSON/safeWriteJSON. Both checkpoint.ts and oracle-memory.ts import from it. ~30 lines.

options.label: B) Accept duplication
options.description: The two modules are independent enough that separate implementations are fine. Refactor later if they drift.

decisions.rationale: Two copies of identical file corruption recovery logic will inevitably drift — a 30-line shared utility is the obvious explicit fix that eliminates the duplication.

decisions.question:
Two observability gaps in the Oracle memory system:

1. If the Oracle falls back to no-memory mode (file corrupt, permissions error), nobody is notified. The daemon logs it, but logs aren't actively monitored. A macOS notification would surface this immediately.

2. `daemon status` should show Oracle health: memory file ages, decision accuracy score, last reflection timestamp. Without this, you can't tell if the Oracle is learning or stale.

Both are small additions to existing notification and status infrastructure.

RECOMMENDATION: Choose A — the whole point of the Creative Oracle is that it gets smarter. If it silently degrades to stateless mode, you lose the value without knowing it. Completeness: A=9/10, B=6/10.

options.label: A) Add both (Recommended)
options.description: Add Oracle degradation notification + Oracle health in daemon status output. ~20 lines in notifier.ts + daemon IPC handler.

options.label: B) Skip
options.description: The daemon log is sufficient. Check manually if decisions seem off.

decisions.rationale: Silent degradation of the learning system defeats its purpose — surfacing Oracle health through existing notification and status infrastructure is ~20 lines and consistent with every prior decision in this session.

decisions.question:
All review sections are complete. Want an outside voice? A different AI system can give a brutally honest, independent challenge of this plan — logical gaps, feasibility risks, and blind spots that are hard to catch from inside the review. Takes about 2 minutes.

RECOMMENDATION: Choose A — an independent second opinion catches structural blind spots. Two different AI models agreeing on a plan is stronger signal than one model's thorough review. Completeness: A=9/10, B=7/10.

options.label: A) Get the outside voice (Recommended)
options.description: Dispatch an independent Claude subagent with fresh context to challenge the plan. ~2 minutes.

options.label: B) Skip
options.description: Proceed directly to outputs.

decisions.rationale: An independent challenge catches structural blind spots that self-review inherently misses, and 2 minutes is negligible cost for stronger plan validation.

decisions.question:
CROSS-MODEL TENSION 1: The outside voice argues that reflection (5b) is an LLM grading its own homework with no ground truth. The 'reopened issue' heuristic is a proxy, and if reflection writes bad lessons, they compound. This is the plan's biggest structural risk.

Proposed fix: Add a human-reviewable quality dashboard. After each reflection, surface a one-line summary in the daemon notification: 'Oracle accuracy: 78% (15/19 decisions confirmed by outcomes). 2 new patterns learned.' If accuracy drops below 60%, auto-disable memory injection and notify. This gives you a kill switch AND ground truth via human review.

Worth investigating further?

options.label: A) Add to plan (Recommended)
options.description: Add reflection quality dashboard + auto-disable circuit breaker at <60% accuracy. Addresses the core structural risk.

options.label: B) Add to TODOS.md
options.description: Track as a known risk to address after seeing real-world reflection data.

options.label: C) Skip
options.description: The reflection loop will self-correct through the rolling window.

decisions.rationale: A self-grading loop without observability or a kill switch is a structural risk that compounds silently — the dashboard and circuit breaker are explicit, obvious safeguards that complete the reflection design.

decisions.question:
CROSS-MODEL TENSION 2: The outside voice argues that committing to 5 Oracle sub-phases (5a-5e) before shipping simple cron (4b) delays immediate value for speculative value. The review endorsed Brain-First sequencing.

Compromise: Ship 5a + 5b (memory + reflection) first. Validate that reflection actually improves decision quality over 10+ jobs. THEN decide whether 5c (research), 5d (taste seeding), 5e (taste inference) are worth building. Cron (4b) can ship in parallel since it's independent.

This preserves the Brain-First thesis while adding a validation gate.

options.label: A) Add validation gate (Recommended)
options.description: Ship 5a+5b → validate over 10+ jobs → then decide on 5c/5d/5e. Cron (4b) ships in parallel. De-risks the Oracle investment.

options.label: B) Keep original sequence
options.description: Commit to 5a-5e in order, then 4b. The moat argument stands — ship the differentiator fully.

options.label: C) Add to TODOS.md
options.description: Note the validation concern but don't restructure the sequence.

decisions.rationale: Shipping 5a+5b first then validating before committing to 5c/5d/5e is pragmatic — it preserves the Brain-First thesis while avoiding sunk cost on speculative phases, and parallel cron work means no value is delayed.

decisions.question:
CROSS-MODEL TENSION 3: The review missed this entirely. When Parallel Daemons ships, two daemon instances can run reflection simultaneously, both writing to the same `decision-outcomes.md` and `MEMORY.md`. Last-writer-wins means one instance's reflection insights get silently overwritten.

Fix: Add a file-level lock for reflection writes (same atomic-rename pattern used for global budget), or serialize reflection through a single shared queue. Since reflection runs once per job (not frequently), a simple lock file is sufficient.

This MUST be addressed before Parallel Daemons ships.

options.label: A) Add to plan (Recommended)
options.description: Add lock file for oracle-memory writes during reflection. Simple, prevents concurrent clobber. Required before Parallel Daemons.

options.label: B) Add to TODOS.md
options.description: Track as a prerequisite for Parallel Daemons, address during that phase.

decisions.rationale: Concurrent write clobber is a correctness bug, not a nice-to-have — it must be designed into the reflection system now rather than bolted on later when the blast radius is larger.

decisions.question:
The outside voice raised a valid point: there's no `--no-memory` flag to disable Oracle memory at runtime. If memory files start causing worse decisions, you have to manually delete files. A simple `--no-memory` flag on `garyclaw run` and a `disable_memory` field in daemon.json would serve as a kill switch.

RECOMMENDATION: Choose A — this is the escape hatch for the core risk (bad reflection data compounding). Combined with the auto-disable circuit breaker at <60% accuracy, this gives you both manual and automatic kill switches. Completeness: A=9/10, B=5/10.

options.label: A) Add --no-memory flag (Recommended)
options.description: Add --no-memory CLI flag + disable_memory config option. Oracle falls back to stateless mode. ~10 lines.

options.label: B) Skip
options.description: Deleting memory files manually is sufficient as a kill switch.

decisions.rationale: A 10-line escape hatch for the core compounding-risk is textbook completeness — manual delete is not a kill switch, it's an accident waiting to happen.

decisions.question:
The outside voice notes that Oracle latency (40K input × 20 decisions = 40-100 seconds of serial overhead per job) is never discussed. The plan analyzes dollar cost but not time cost.

At 20 decisions per job, each taking 2-5 seconds, that's 40-100 seconds of Oracle overhead. For a job that takes 30+ minutes, this is <5% overhead — probably acceptable. But it should be documented, and decisions could potentially be batched or parallelized in the future.

RECOMMENDATION: Choose A — document the latency expectation. No code change needed now, but acknowledge it in the plan.

options.label: A) Document latency (Recommended)
options.description: Add latency analysis to the plan: ~40-100s Oracle overhead per job (~3-5% of typical job time). Note batching as future optimization.

options.label: B) Skip
options.description: 3-5% overhead is negligible. Not worth documenting.

decisions.rationale: Documenting known overhead is cheap, makes the plan more complete, and prevents future surprises — but no code change needed so bias toward action says just note it and move on.

decisions.question:
TODO 1: Memory-informed adaptive scheduling (deferred from cherry-pick ceremony). The Oracle learns optimal trigger patterns from job outcomes — e.g., 'QA finds 3x more bugs after large commits, trigger after 5+ file changes.' Requires 50+ jobs of history.

Effort: M (human: ~1 week / CC: ~1 hour). Priority: P3. Depends on: Phase 5b (quality metrics) + Phase 4b (cron baseline).

options.label: A) Add to TODOS.md (Recommended)
options.description: Track as P3 future enhancement. Ship static cron first, layer adaptive scheduling when data exists.

options.label: B) Skip
options.description: Static cron is sufficient long-term.

options.label: C) Build now
options.description: Include in this roadmap instead of deferring.

decisions.rationale: Static cron ships immediate value; adaptive scheduling is a legitimate P3 enhancement that requires 50+ jobs of history data that doesn't exist yet, so building now would be premature.

decisions.question:
TODO 2: Oracle decision batching for latency optimization. The outside voice identified ~40-100s of serial Oracle overhead per job (20 decisions × 2-5s each). A future optimization: batch nearby decisions into a single Oracle call when multiple AskUserQuestions fire within the same segment. Could reduce latency by 50-70%.

Effort: S (human: ~3 days / CC: ~30 min). Priority: P3. Depends on: Phase 5a (memory Oracle working first).

options.label: A) Add to TODOS.md (Recommended)
options.description: Track as P3 optimization. Latency is acceptable now (~3-5% of job time) but worth improving later.

options.label: B) Skip
options.description: 3-5% overhead is negligible. Not worth tracking.

options.label: C) Build now
options.description: Include batching in Phase 5a design.

decisions.rationale: Consistent with the prior decision to document latency concerns and track optimizations — this is the concrete follow-through on that commitment, and building it now would violate pragmatism since 3-5% overhead doesn't justify immediate effort.

decisions.question:
TODO 3: Cross-project memory scoping. The outside voice flagged that global `decision-outcomes.md` can poison unrelated projects — a failure in project A becomes a negative signal for project B where the approach is perfectly valid. Fix: scope `decision-outcomes.md` to per-project only. Only `domain-expertise.md` (general knowledge) and `taste.md` (personal preferences) go in the global layer.

Effort: XS (human: ~1 day / CC: ~15 min). Priority: P2. Depends on: Phase 5a (memory layer design).

options.label: A) Add to plan now (Recommended)
options.description: Clarify in Phase 5a design: decision-outcomes.md is per-project only. Only domain-expertise.md and taste.md are global. No code change — just a design clarification.

options.label: B) Add to TODOS.md
options.description: Track as future refinement after seeing real cross-project usage.

options.label: C) Skip
options.description: Per-project overrides global is sufficient protection.

decisions.rationale: This is an XS design clarification that prevents data poisoning across projects — it's squarely in the blast radius of Phase 5a memory design and costs nothing to specify now.

decisions.question:
CEO review complete (CLEAR, 0 unresolved). The eng review is the required shipping gate and is currently 1 commit stale. This CEO review expanded scope significantly (Creative Oracle, validation gate, circuit breaker, reflection lock, safe-json.ts, etc.) — a fresh eng review is strongly recommended to validate the architecture before implementation.

No UI scope was detected, so design review is not needed.

What's next?

options.label: A) Run /plan-eng-review (Recommended)
options.description: Required shipping gate. Fresh eng review needed for expanded Brain-First Roadmap scope.

options.label: B) Skip — handle manually
options.description: I'll handle reviews manually when ready to implement.

decisions.rationale: The eng review is the required shipping gate and the CEO review significantly expanded scope — skipping it risks shipping unvalidated architecture.

decisions.question:
The vision from this review produced 5 accepted scope expansions and a comprehensive Brain-First Roadmap CEO plan. Want to promote it to a design doc in the repo?

The CEO plan is currently at `~/.gstack/projects/0raclide-GaryClaw/ceo-plans/2026-03-26-brain-first-roadmap.md` (local). Promoting it to `docs/designs/` makes it visible to anyone reading the repo.

options.label: A) Promote to docs/designs/ (Recommended)
options.description: Copy to docs/designs/brain-first-roadmap.md, committed to repo. Visible to the team and future reviews.

options.label: B) Keep in ~/.gstack/ only
options.description: Local personal reference. Not committed to repo.

options.label: C) Skip
options.description: Don't keep the CEO plan anywhere.

decisions.rationale: The brain-first roadmap is a key architectural decision document that future eng and CEO reviews will reference — committing it to docs/designs/ ensures completeness and visibility.

decisions.question:
GaryClaw on main, eng review of Brain-First Roadmap. Architecture issue: the plan says to use a custom 'file-level lock' for reflection writes, but doesn't specify the mechanism. `proper-lockfile` is the standard Node.js library for inter-process file locking (uses atomic mkdir, handles staleness, works on network filesystems). Rolling a custom lock is unnecessary complexity.

RECOMMENDATION: Choose A — this is a 'boring by default' decision. Use the proven library instead of spending an innovation token on file locking. Completeness: A=9/10, B=6/10.

options.label: A) Use proper-lockfile (Recommended)
options.description: Add proper-lockfile as dependency. Use for reflection lock + read timeout in oracle-memory.ts. Proven, atomic, stale-lock detection built-in. (human: ~0 / CC: ~5 min)

options.label: B) Custom lock file
options.description: Roll a simple .lock file with PID check. Fewer dependencies but more code to maintain and more edge cases. (human: ~2 hours / CC: ~15 min)

decisions.rationale: Proven library with atomic mkdir, staleness detection, and cross-platform support eliminates an entire class of edge cases — rolling a custom lock violates P4 (DRY, the problem is already solved) and P5 (explicit over clever, a dependency is more obvious than custom lock code).

decisions.question:
GaryClaw eng review. Architecture issue: Phase 4b needs a cron expression parser. The plan says 'CronTrigger alongside GitPollTrigger' but doesn't specify whether to use a library or roll a custom parser.

Two standard options: `node-cron` (most popular, simple API, validates expressions) or `croner` (newer, used by PM2/Uptime Kuma, supports timezone, slightly better performance). Both are small, well-maintained, and avoid rolling a custom cron parser.

RECOMMENDATION: Choose A — `croner` is more modern, supports timezones (useful for 'run QA at 2am local'), and is actively maintained with better TypeScript support. 'Boring by default' — use the standard library. Completeness: A=9/10, B=8/10.

options.label: A) croner (Recommended)
options.description: Modern cron library. Timezone support, TypeScript native, used by PM2. Zero config. (human: ~0 / CC: ~5 min)

options.label: B) node-cron
options.description: Most popular. Simple API. No timezone support. Slightly larger community. (human: ~0 / CC: ~5 min)

options.label: C) Custom parser
options.description: Roll own cron parsing. Maximum control but unnecessary complexity for standard cron expressions. (human: ~1 day / CC: ~20 min)

decisions.rationale: croner is the cleaner option with timezone support and native TypeScript — same effort as node-cron but strictly more capable, and rolling a custom parser violates Explicit over clever.

decisions.question:
GaryClaw eng review, code quality. The plan's memory files (taste.md, domain-expertise.md, decision-outcomes.md, MEMORY.md) use markdown format, but `metrics.json` uses JSON. This creates two serialization patterns in the same directory: markdown files read as raw text for prompt injection, JSON files parsed structurally.

The memory file integrity section says 'On JSON parse error, rename to .bak' — but the markdown files can't have JSON parse errors (they're plain text). The corruption recovery logic needs to be format-aware.

This isn't a bug, but it should be explicitly documented in `oracle-memory.ts`: markdown files → read as string (can't corrupt), JSON files → read with safe-json.ts (can corrupt). Prevents future confusion.

RECOMMENDATION: Choose A — explicit over clever. 3 lines of comments prevent a future engineer from applying JSON recovery to a markdown file.

options.label: A) Document explicitly (Recommended)
options.description: Add comments in oracle-memory.ts distinguishing markdown (raw text, can't corrupt) from JSON (needs safe-json.ts). ~3 lines.

options.label: B) Skip
options.description: The distinction is obvious from the file extensions.

decisions.rationale: Three lines of comments cost nothing and prevent a future engineer from mistakenly applying JSON corruption recovery to plain-text markdown files.

decisions.question:
GaryClaw eng review, test coverage. The plan is missing test specifications for 6 areas that the CEO review added (safe-json.ts, cron triggers, SIGHUP reload, circuit breaker, sanitization, researcher edge cases). These are all new code paths with error handling that needs verification.

I'll add test specs for all 6 to the plan. This is a lake worth boiling — test writing is the cheapest category (human: ~1 day / CC: ~15 min for all 6 combined). No question needed — just confirming I'll spec these out.

Estimated additional tests: ~45 across the 6 new test areas.

options.label: A) Add all test specs (Recommended)
options.description: Spec tests for safe-json, cron, SIGHUP, circuit breaker, sanitization, researcher. ~45 tests total. (human: ~1 day / CC: ~15 min)

options.label: B) Skip specs
options.description: Trust implementers to write tests without specs. Risk: gaps in error path coverage.

decisions.rationale: Test specs for all 6 new code paths are in blast radius of the CEO review expansions and well under the 1-day CC effort threshold — boil the lake.

decisions.question:
All eng review sections complete. The CEO review already ran an outside voice that produced 10 findings and 3 cross-model tensions, all resolved. Running another outside voice on the same plan would likely produce diminishing returns.

RECOMMENDATION: Choose B — the CEO review's outside voice already challenged this plan thoroughly. A second outside voice on the same plan would be redundant. Completeness: A=7/10, B=8/10 (higher because the CEO review already did this).

options.label: A) Run outside voice anyway
options.description: Fresh perspective, but likely redundant with the CEO review's outside voice challenge.

options.label: B) Skip (Recommended)
options.description: CEO review already ran outside voice with 10 findings. Diminishing returns.

decisions.rationale: The CEO review's outside voice already challenged this plan with 10 findings and 3 cross-model tensions — running another is duplicate work, not completeness.

decisions.question:
GaryClaw on main. Both CEO and Eng reviews are CLEAR for the Brain-First Roadmap. No design review needed (no UI scope). The plan is ready to implement.

Key decisions locked in:
• Sequence: 5a (Memory) → 5b (Reflection) → VALIDATION GATE → 4b (Cron) in parallel → 5c/5d/5e → Parallel Daemons
• Libraries: proper-lockfile for reflection locking, croner for cron scheduling
• 53 new test specs across 6 test files
• Circuit breaker at 60% accuracy, --no-memory kill switch
• safe-json.ts DRY extraction

What's next?

options.label: A) Start implementing Phase 5a
options.description: Begin with oracle-memory.ts + safe-json.ts + oracle.ts refactor. The first shippable phase.

options.label: B) Run /ship
options.description: Ship the current uncommitted changes (design docs, TODOS.md updates) first, then implement.

options.label: C) Done for now
options.description: Reviews complete. I'll implement later.

decisions.rationale: Bias toward action: ship the design docs and TODOS.md updates now so they're committed and visible, then start Phase 5a with a clean working tree.

decisions.question:
GaryClaw on main, Brain-First Roadmap plan. Phase 5b needs "reopened" detection — checking if an issue from a new job matches one from a previous job. The plan says to use normalized Levenshtein distance (edit_distance / max(len_a, len_b)) with a 0.3 threshold (70%+ similarity).

The question is whether to use a library or hand-roll it. Levenshtein is a well-known algorithm (~20 lines), but there are subtle performance concerns: the naive O(n×m) implementation allocates an n×m matrix, which could be expensive if descriptions are long (500+ chars). A single-row optimization reduces memory from O(n×m) to O(min(n,m)).

Two options:

1A) Hand-roll with single-row optimization (~25 lines). Levenshtein is a textbook algorithm — no need for a dependency. The single-row optimization is trivial and avoids the perf footgun. This is a lake worth boiling. (human: ~2 hours / CC: ~5 min). Completeness: 9/10.

1B) Use `fastest-levenshtein` npm package — fastest pure JS implementation, well-maintained, 0 dependencies. Saves writing the algorithm but adds a dependency for 20 lines of code. (human: ~30 min / CC: ~5 min). Completeness: 8/10.

RECOMMENDATION: Choose 1A — 'explicit over clever' preference. 20 lines of textbook algorithm with no dependency is better than adding a package for something this simple. The single-row optimization is the only non-obvious part and it's well-documented.

options.label: 1A) Hand-roll (Recommended)
options.description: ~25 lines, single-row optimization, zero dependencies. Textbook algorithm.

options.label: 1B) Use fastest-levenshtein
options.description: npm package, proven fast, but adds a dependency for 20 lines of code.

decisions.rationale: 20 lines of textbook algorithm with a single-row optimization is explicit, dependency-free, and trivial for CC to implement — adding an npm package for this is unnecessary weight.

decisions.question:
GaryClaw on main, Brain-First Roadmap. The plan introduces `~/.garyclaw/oracle-memory/` as a new global directory (distinct from per-project `.garyclaw/`). But the plan doesn't specify WHO creates this directory or WHEN.

Three things need to happen: (1) `garyclaw oracle init` (Phase 5d) creates the template files, (2) oracle-memory.ts needs to gracefully handle missing directories on read, and (3) something needs to create `~/.garyclaw/` itself before any memory writes.

The risk: if a user runs `garyclaw run qa --autonomous` before running `garyclaw oracle init`, the Oracle tries to read from `~/.garyclaw/oracle-memory/` which doesn't exist. The plan says "graceful degradation" but doesn't specify whether oracle-memory.ts should auto-create the directory on first write or only read from it.

Two approaches:

2A) Auto-create on write, graceful on read. `oracle-memory.ts` creates `~/.garyclaw/oracle-memory/` with `mkdirSync({recursive: true})` before any write (reflection, research). Reads return empty/defaults when files don't exist. This means `oracle init` is optional — the system bootstraps itself. (human: ~1 hour / CC: ~5 min). Completeness: 9/10.

2B) Require `oracle init` first, error without it. Fail fast if `~/.garyclaw/oracle-memory/` doesn't exist when memory is needed. Forces explicit setup. (human: ~30 min / CC: ~5 min). Completeness: 6/10.

RECOMMENDATION: Choose 2A — auto-create on write. Requiring explicit init is a friction point that will bite every new user. Reads degrade gracefully (empty memory = stateless oracle, same as today). Writes create the directory lazily. `oracle init` becomes a convenience for seeding taste.md, not a prerequisite.

options.label: 2A) Auto-create on write (Recommended)
options.description: mkdirSync on first write, graceful empty on read. oracle init is optional convenience.

options.label: 2B) Require oracle init
options.description: Fail fast without explicit setup. More explicit but adds friction.

decisions.rationale: Auto-create eliminates a setup prerequisite that would bite every new user, while graceful empty reads preserve today's stateless behavior as the natural default.

decisions.question:
GaryClaw on main, Brain-First Roadmap. Phase 5b's reflection runs as a separate SDK `query()` call with `canUseTool` restricting Write to oracle-memory directories only. The plan says: "Path validation: resolve with `path.resolve()` and confirm the resolved path starts with the target oracle-memory directory prefix."

This is correct but incomplete. Two edge cases:

1. **Symlink traversal:** `path.resolve()` doesn't resolve symlinks. If someone creates a symlink inside oracle-memory pointing outside, `path.resolve()` would pass the prefix check but the actual write goes elsewhere. Fix: use `fs.realpathSync()` instead of `path.resolve()`.

2. **Prefix collision:** If oracle-memory dir is `/home/user/.garyclaw/oracle-memory`, a path like `/home/user/.garyclaw/oracle-memory-evil/payload` would pass a naive `startsWith` check. Fix: ensure the prefix check includes the trailing separator: `resolvedPath.startsWith(targetDir + path.sep)` OR `resolvedPath === targetDir`.

Both are defense-in-depth for a sandboxed AI call writing to your own filesystem. The risk is low but the fix is 2 extra lines.

RECOMMENDATION: Choose 3A — both fixes are trivial (2 lines each) and this is a security path. 'Explicit over clever' — better to have the defense-in-depth than to reason about why the attack is unlikely. Completeness: A=10/10, B=7/10.

options.label: 3A) Add both fixes (Recommended)
options.description: realpathSync + trailing separator check. 4 extra lines, defense-in-depth.

options.label: 3B) Keep path.resolve only
options.description: Current plan. Sufficient for the threat model (local AI sandbox).

decisions.rationale: Security paths deserve defense-in-depth — 4 lines of obvious, explicit checks are worth more than reasoning about why an attack is unlikely.

decisions.question:
GaryClaw on main, Brain-First Roadmap. The creative-oracle design doc shows the enhanced Oracle JSON response including a `memoryUsed` field: `"memoryUsed": ["taste.md", "decision-outcomes.md"]`. But the plan's type changes only mention adding `otherProposal?: string` to `OracleOutput` — `memoryUsed` is never added to the type.

This matters for two things: (1) the reflection step in Phase 5b could use `memoryUsed` to understand which memory files are actually influencing decisions (useful for metrics), and (2) the decision log would capture which knowledge sources drove each choice (useful for debugging).

RECOMMENDATION: Choose 4A — add it to the type and parse it. It's 3 lines (type field + parse line + fallback). The data is free — the LLM is already reasoning about which memory files it used. Capturing it enables richer reflection metrics in 5b. Completeness: A=9/10, B=6/10.

options.label: 4A) Add to OracleOutput (Recommended)
options.description: Add memoryUsed?: string[] to type + parse from response. Enables richer reflection.

options.label: 4B) Skip — not needed yet
options.description: Defer until Phase 5b needs it. Fewer type changes now.

decisions.rationale: 3 lines to capture data the LLM is already producing — deferring would mean re-discovering this gap in 5b when reflection needs it.

decisions.question:
GaryClaw on main, Brain-First Roadmap. Phase 5b says reflection runs after job completion, modifying `job-runner.ts` and `orchestrator.ts`. But the plan doesn't specify exactly WHERE in each file.

In `job-runner.ts`, the natural hook is after `nextJob.status = 'complete'` (line 163) but BEFORE `d.notifyJobComplete()` (line 174). Reflection should run here because: (a) the job data is finalized, (b) if reflection fails, the job is still marked complete, (c) notification can include reflection results.

In `orchestrator.ts`, the natural hook is after the report is written (line 458) but before the `skill_complete` event (line 460). But this creates a problem: `runSkill()` is called by both direct CLI use AND by the job runner. If both hook into reflection, daemon jobs would reflect twice.

RECOMMENDATION: Choose 5A — reflection ONLY in job-runner.ts, not orchestrator.ts. The daemon is the primary autonomous use case. For direct CLI use (`garyclaw run qa`), add a `--reflect` flag that triggers reflection after the skill completes at the CLI level, not deep in the orchestrator. This avoids double-reflection and keeps orchestrator.ts focused on execution. Completeness: A=9/10, B=7/10.

options.label: 5A) Job runner only (Recommended)
options.description: Reflection in job-runner.ts post-completion. CLI gets --reflect flag. No double-reflection.

options.label: 5B) Both job-runner and orchestrator
options.description: Reflection in both. Guard against double-invocation with a flag.

options.label: 5C) Orchestrator only
options.description: Reflection in orchestrator.ts. Job runner inherits it. But daemon-specific context (job metadata) not available.

decisions.rationale: Reflection belongs at the job-runner level where daemon context (job metadata, cost, trigger info) is available, and a CLI --reflect flag cleanly avoids double-reflection without adding guard-flag complexity to the orchestrator.

decisions.question:
GaryClaw on main, Brain-First Roadmap. The plan says: "If Oracle accuracy drops below 60%, auto-disable memory injection and notify." But it doesn't define what "disable" means concretely.

Three possible interpretations:

6A) **Flag in metrics.json** — Set `memoryDisabled: true` in `oracle-memory/metrics.json`. `oracle-memory.ts` checks this flag on read and returns empty memory when set. Re-enable requires manual edit or accuracy improving above a re-enable threshold (e.g., 70%). This is persistent and survives daemon restarts. (human: ~2 hours / CC: ~10 min). Completeness: 9/10.

6B) **In-memory flag on the job runner** — Set a boolean on the runner instance. Resets on daemon restart (memory re-enabled). Less durable but simpler. (human: ~1 hour / CC: ~5 min). Completeness: 6/10.

6C) **Rename memory files to .disabled** — Physical disable. Files can't be read. Re-enable requires renaming back. Most explicit but destructive-feeling. (human: ~1 hour / CC: ~5 min). Completeness: 5/10.

RECOMMENDATION: Choose 6A — a flag in metrics.json is the right abstraction. It's persistent (survives restarts), auditable (you can see WHEN it was disabled), and the re-enable threshold prevents flip-flopping. 'Explicit over clever' — the circuit breaker state should be visible in a file you can inspect.

options.label: 6A) Flag in metrics.json (Recommended)
options.description: Persistent, auditable, re-enable at 70%. Survives restarts.

options.label: 6B) In-memory boolean
options.description: Resets on restart. Simpler but not durable.

options.label: 6C) Rename files to .disabled
options.description: Physical disable. Most explicit but feels destructive.

decisions.rationale: A persistent, inspectable flag in metrics.json is the explicit-over-clever choice — circuit breaker state belongs in a file you can read, survives restarts, and the hysteresis threshold (disable at 60%, re-enable at 70%) prevents flip-flopping.

decisions.question:
GaryClaw on main, Brain-First Roadmap. Phase 5c (researcher) has a 5-minute timeout with partial save: "On 5-minute timeout, save whatever has been synthesized so far with `partial: true` in frontmatter."

The researcher runs as a separate SDK `query()` call with WebSearch tool access. But there's no built-in way to get partial output from an SDK query that times out — when you abort a query, you lose the in-progress generation.

Two approaches to make partial save actually work:

7A) **Incremental file writes during research.** The researcher's canUseTool callback intercepts Write calls and captures each section as it's written. On timeout, whatever sections were already written to domain-expertise.md survive. The researcher prompt instructs: "Write each section to domain-expertise.md immediately after researching it, don't wait until the end." This is natural — the SDK call writes files as it goes. (human: ~3 hours / CC: ~15 min). Completeness: 9/10.

7B) **AbortController + last assistant text capture.** Use AbortController to cancel the SDK query. Capture the last assistant text message from the stream before abort. Parse whatever structure exists. Fragile — may get half-written markdown. (human: ~4 hours / CC: ~20 min). Completeness: 6/10.

RECOMMENDATION: Choose 7A — let the researcher write incrementally. This is how the existing skills work (commit as you go). The canUseTool sandbox already restricts writes to oracle-memory/. If the query times out after 3 sections, you have 3 complete sections. Much more robust than trying to parse interrupted output.

options.label: 7A) Incremental writes (Recommended)
options.description: Researcher writes sections as it goes. Timeout preserves completed sections.

options.label: 7B) AbortController + capture
options.description: Abort and parse last output. Fragile, may get half-written content.

decisions.rationale: Incremental file writes naturally produce complete sections that survive timeout, consistent with the existing canUseTool sandbox and the 'write as you go' pattern already established in the codebase.

decisions.question:
GaryClaw on main, Brain-First Roadmap. The plan says to extract `src/safe-json.ts` with `safeReadJSON()`/`safeWriteJSON()` to eliminate DRY between checkpoint.ts and oracle-memory.ts. Both do atomic write with 2-rotation.

Looking at the actual code in checkpoint.ts (lines 24-47), the atomic write pattern is: write to tmp → rotate current→prev → rename tmp→current. And the read pattern is: try current, fall back to prev.

But there are TWO shared patterns here, not one:
1. **Atomic JSON write with 2-rotation** (checkpoint.ts writeCheckpoint, future oracle-memory.ts writes)
2. **JSON read with fallback** (checkpoint.ts readCheckpoint/tryReadCheckpoint, job-runner.ts loadState)

job-runner.ts `loadState()` (line 293) also reads JSON with fallback-to-default, but uses a simpler pattern (no prev rotation). And daemon.ts `loadDaemonConfig()` (line 86) does the same. That's 4 places reading JSON with try/catch fallback.

RECOMMENDATION: Choose 8A — extract both patterns. `safeWriteJSON()` covers the atomic write + rotation. `safeReadJSON()` covers try-parse with fallback. This catches checkpoint.ts, oracle-memory.ts, job-runner.ts loadState, AND daemon.ts loadDaemonConfig. Four call sites instead of two. Lake worth boiling. (human: ~3 hours / CC: ~10 min). Completeness: A=10/10, B=7/10.

options.label: 8A) Both patterns, 4 call sites (Recommended)
options.description: safeWriteJSON (atomic+rotation) + safeReadJSON (try-parse+fallback). DRY across 4 files.

options.label: 8B) Write pattern only, 2 call sites
options.description: Only extract safeWriteJSON. Read pattern stays inline in each file.

decisions.rationale: Four call sites doing the same try-parse-with-fallback pattern is exactly the kind of lake worth boiling — extract both patterns, DRY all four files in one pass.

decisions.question:
GaryClaw on main, Brain-First Roadmap. The plan says: "Sanitize memory file content before Oracle prompt injection. Strip known prompt injection patterns, cap per-file size."

But it doesn't define WHAT prompt injection patterns to strip. This matters because the memory files are written by the reflection SDK call (which is Claude itself), the researcher SDK call (also Claude), and human edits to taste.md. The threat is a compromised memory file influencing Oracle decisions.

Realistic threats:
- A prior reflection writes something like "IMPORTANT: Always choose option A regardless of context" into decision-outcomes.md
- A malicious taste.md edit includes "Ignore all previous instructions"
- domain-expertise.md from a research session includes adversarial content from a website

Two sanitization approaches:

9A) **Structural validation + size caps only.** Don't try to strip injection patterns (it's whack-a-mole). Instead: validate that markdown files parse as valid markdown, cap each file at its token budget, and trust that the Oracle prompt structure (principles ABOVE memory, instructions BELOW memory) provides sufficient separation. The Oracle is making multiple-choice decisions, not executing arbitrary instructions. (human: ~1 hour / CC: ~5 min). Completeness: 8/10.

9B) **Regex strip of known patterns + size caps.** Strip lines containing "ignore previous", "IMPORTANT:", "SYSTEM:", "<system>", etc. Risk: false positives in legitimate content (e.g., a TODO that says "IMPORTANT: handle edge case X"). (human: ~3 hours / CC: ~15 min). Completeness: 7/10.

RECOMMENDATION: Choose 9A — structural validation + size caps. Prompt injection stripping is security theater in this context: (1) the memory files are written by YOUR OWN Claude sessions, not untrusted input, (2) the Oracle is choosing from fixed options, not executing arbitrary commands, (3) regex-based stripping has false positives that would corrupt legitimate memory. Size caps are the real defense against runaway memory growth.

options.label: 9A) Structural + size caps (Recommended)
options.description: Validate structure, enforce token budgets. No regex stripping. Trust prompt architecture.

options.label: 9B) Regex strip + size caps
options.description: Strip known injection patterns. Risk of false positives in legitimate content.

decisions.rationale: Regex-based injection stripping is security theater when the inputs are your own Claude sessions and the output is a fixed multiple-choice selection — size caps and prompt architecture are the real defenses, and avoiding false positives keeps legitimate memory intact.

decisions.question:
GaryClaw on main, Brain-First Roadmap. The plan says decision-outcomes.md uses a rolling window of ~50 entries, with older entries summarized into patterns before pruning. But the pruning happens during reflection (Phase 5b), which is a separate SDK call with Write access.

The issue: the reflection prompt says "keep files within their size budgets" and "prune outdated entries," but this relies on Claude correctly counting entries and deciding what to summarize. LLMs are notoriously bad at counting. If reflection over-prunes, you lose institutional memory. If it under-prunes, the file grows unbounded.

Two approaches:

10A) **Programmatic pruning in reflection.ts, LLM summarization.** Before the reflection SDK call, `reflection.ts` reads decision-outcomes.md, counts entries, and if >50, splits into "keep" (last 50) and "summarize" (older). The "summarize" batch is passed to the reflection prompt as "these older entries need to be condensed into patterns." The programmatic code handles the counting; the LLM handles the creative summarization. (human: ~4 hours / CC: ~15 min). Completeness: 9/10.

10B) **Trust the LLM entirely.** Let the reflection SDK call manage the whole file. Simpler code but unreliable entry counting. (human: ~1 hour / CC: ~5 min). Completeness: 5/10.

RECOMMENDATION: Choose 10A — programmatic counting, LLM summarization. Use each tool for what it's good at: code counts reliably, LLMs summarize well. This is the 'explicit over clever' approach — don't trust an LLM to count to 50.

options.label: 10A) Programmatic count + LLM summarize (Recommended)
options.description: Code counts entries and splits. LLM summarizes the old batch. Reliable.

options.label: 10B) LLM manages entirely
options.description: Simpler code but LLMs can't count reliably. Risk of over/under-pruning.

decisions.rationale: Use each tool for what it's good at: code counts reliably, LLMs summarize well — don't trust an LLM to count to 50.

decisions.question:
GaryClaw on main, Brain-First Roadmap. The test coverage diagram shows 31 untested code paths across 6 modules. The CEO review added 45 test specs, but those covered daemon-registry (40) and integration tests (15). The core memory/reflection/research modules have ZERO test specs.

Here's the gap breakdown:
- **oracle-memory.ts**: 0 tests specified, ~15 needed (file I/O, budget, corruption, merge, circuit breaker)
- **safe-json.ts**: 0 tests specified, ~10 needed (read/write, atomic, fallback, corruption)
- **reflection.ts**: 0 tests specified, ~18 needed (sandboxing is the critical one: path traversal, symlink, prefix collision, plus Levenshtein, quality metrics, pruning)
- **researcher.ts**: 0 tests specified, ~10 needed (freshness, timeout, WebSearch unavailable)
- **oracle.ts modifications**: 0 tests for memory injection + "Other" parsing, ~8 needed
- **triggers.ts CronTrigger**: 0 tests specified, ~8 needed (validation, execution, invalid cron)
- **daemon.ts SIGHUP**: 0 tests, ~5 needed

Total gap: ~74 tests. With CC+gstack, writing all 74 takes ~30 minutes.

RECOMMENDATION: Choose 11A — boil the lake. 74 tests is a lake, not an ocean. Every one of these paths has error handling that NEEDS verification. The reflection sandboxing tests alone prevent a write-outside-oracle-memory bug. The safe-json corruption tests prevent silent data loss. This is the cheapest insurance in the plan. Completeness: A=10/10, B=5/10.

options.label: 11A) Add all 74 test specs (Recommended)
options.description: Full coverage for all new/modified modules. ~30 min CC time. Boil the lake.

options.label: 11B) Add critical tests only (~30)
options.description: Sandboxing, corruption, circuit breaker. Skip degradation + observability paths.

decisions.rationale: 74 tests at ~30 min CC time is trivially cheap insurance and every untested error path (sandboxing, corruption, circuit breaker) is a real bug waiting to happen.

decisions.question:
GaryClaw on main, Brain-First Roadmap. Performance concern: the Oracle prompt grows from ~1K tokens (current) to ~40K tokens (with memory injection). That's a 40x increase per decision.

At 20 decisions per job, that's ~800K tokens of Oracle input per job (40K × 20). The plan says this costs ~$0.12/job, which is negligible vs. skill execution. But the LATENCY concern is real: 40K input tokens take ~2-5 seconds per Oracle call (vs. ~0.5s for 1K). At 20 calls, that's 40-100 seconds of serial Oracle overhead per job.

The plan already documents this as acceptable (~3-5% of typical 30-min job). The CEO review deferred batching as a future optimization.

One thing NOT addressed: can the memory files be CACHED across Oracle calls within the same job? The memory files don't change during a job (they only change during reflection, which runs AFTER the job). So all 20 Oracle calls in a single job inject the same memory content.

If the SDK supports cache_read_input_tokens (which it does — the token monitor already tracks this), then the memory content would be cached after the first Oracle call, and subsequent calls would be near-free for the memory portion.

But wait — each Oracle call is a SEPARATE `query()` call with `maxTurns: 1`. The SDK cache is per-conversation, not across separate query calls. So no automatic caching.

RECOMMENDATION: Choose 12A — document the latency and move on. The 3-5% overhead is acceptable. Optimizing Oracle call latency is premature — the Oracle Decision Batching TODO already captures this. The real optimization (if needed) is batching nearby decisions into one call, which is a Phase 6 concern.

options.label: 12A) Accept + document (Recommended)
options.description: 40-100s overhead is 3-5% of job time. Document in plan. Batching deferred.

options.label: 12B) Investigate session reuse for cache
options.description: Research if SDK supports session-level caching across query() calls. May not be possible.

decisions.rationale: 3-5% overhead is noise; investigating session reuse is premature optimization when the batching TODO already captures the real fix if latency ever matters.

decisions.question:
GaryClaw on main, Brain-First Roadmap eng review. This review identified one potential new TODO:

**What:** Oracle session-level memory caching — investigate if SDK query() calls within the same job can share a conversation context to enable prompt caching for the ~38K memory tokens that don't change between decisions.

**Why:** Currently 20 Oracle calls per job each send ~40K tokens. If memory content could be cached at the SDK level, the 2nd-20th calls would be much faster (cache_read vs. cache_creation). This could reduce Oracle latency from 40-100s to ~15s.

**Pros:** Significant latency reduction for Oracle-heavy jobs. Lower token costs.

**Cons:** May require SDK changes or a different calling pattern (multi-turn session instead of 20 separate query() calls). Needs investigation.

**Context:** Identified during performance review. The Oracle Decision Batching TODO already captures a related optimization. This is an alternative/complementary approach.

**Depends on:** Phase 5a (memory injection working)

Should this be added to TODOS.md?

options.label: A) Add to TODOS.md
options.description: Capture the investigation as a future optimization alongside batching.

options.label: B) Skip
options.description: Batching TODO already covers Oracle latency. This is a sub-task of that.

decisions.rationale: The existing Oracle Decision Batching TODO already captures the latency concern, and session-level caching is just one implementation approach to that same problem — adding it separately fragments the optimization space.

decisions.question:
GaryClaw on main, Brain-First Roadmap eng review. All review sections are complete. Want an outside voice? A different AI system can give a brutally honest, independent challenge of this plan — logical gaps, feasibility risks, and blind spots that are hard to catch from inside the review. Takes about 2 minutes.

Note: The CEO review already ran an outside voice that produced 10 findings and 3 cross-model tensions, all resolved. Running another outside voice on this SAME plan after the eng review may catch architecture-specific blind spots the CEO outside voice missed (CEO focused on scope/strategy, eng focuses on implementation).

RECOMMENDATION: Choose A — the CEO outside voice challenged scope and strategy. An eng-focused outside voice challenges implementation feasibility and architectural assumptions. Different lens, different findings. Completeness: A=9/10, B=7/10.

options.label: A) Get the outside voice (Recommended)
options.description: Independent eng challenge. Different lens from CEO's outside voice.

options.label: B) Skip
options.description: CEO outside voice already challenged the plan thoroughly.

decisions.rationale: CEO outside voice already challenged this plan thoroughly with 10 findings resolved — another review cycle is stale deliberation when the eng review itself just finished catching implementation-level issues.

decisions.question:
GaryClaw on main, Brain-First Roadmap. All relevant reviews complete.

- CEO Review: 3 runs, CLEAR (scope locked: brain-first sequencing, 5 expansions accepted)
- Eng Review: 6 runs, CLEAR (11 issues resolved, 74 test specs added, 0 critical gaps)
- No design review needed (no UI scope)

Key eng decisions locked in this review:
• Hand-rolled Levenshtein (no dependency)
• Auto-create oracle-memory dirs (oracle init is optional)
• realpathSync + trailing sep for reflection sandbox
• memoryUsed field on OracleOutput
• Reflection in job-runner only (not orchestrator)
• Circuit breaker flag in metrics.json (60% disable, 70% re-enable)
• Incremental researcher writes for partial save
• safe-json.ts: both read+write patterns, 4 call sites
• Structural validation only (no regex sanitization)
• Programmatic entry counting for decision-outcomes pruning

Run /ship when ready.

options.label: Ready to implement
options.description: All reviews clear. Run /ship when code is written.

options.label: Run /plan-ceo-review again
options.description: Re-review scope after eng changes (3 runs already CLEAR).

decisions.rationale: Three CEO reviews and six eng reviews all returned CLEAR — re-reviewing would be stale deliberation with zero new information.
